stem: \( f'(x)=x^{2}-([[s]])x+([[p]]) \). \( f \) changes from increasing to decreasing at one of its critical points; give a critical \( x \).
solution: The sign of \( f' \) changes at its roots \( [[r1]],[[r2]] \); one is \( [[answer]] \).

stem: Let \( f(x)=x^{3}+([[B]])x^{2} \). On the interval where \( f \) is BOTH decreasing and concave down, what is the right endpoint of that interval?
solution: \( f'(x)=3x^{2}+2([[B]])x=x(3x+2\cdot[[B]]) \) is zero at \( x=0 \) and \( x=-\frac{2\cdot[[B]]}{3} \), so \( f \) is decreasing on \( \left(0,-\frac{2\cdot[[B]]}{3}\right) \). \( f''(x)=6x+2\cdot[[B]] \) is zero at \( x=-\frac{[[B]]}{3} \), so \( f \) is concave down for \( x<-\frac{[[B]]}{3} \). Intersecting, \( f \) is decreasing and concave down on \( \left(0,-\frac{[[B]]}{3}\right) \), whose right endpoint is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the right endpoint of the full decreasing interval, ignoring the concavity condition.
This drops the factor of 3 in the inflection-point formula.
This is the left endpoint of the interval, not the right endpoint.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f'(x) = (x-([[r1]]))(x-([[r2]]))(x-([[r3]])) \), where \( [[r1]]<[[r2]]<[[r3]] \). By tracking the sign of \( f' \) across all three critical points, at which one does \( f \) have a local MAXIMUM?
solution: For \( x<[[r1]] \), all three factors are negative so \( f'<0 \) (decreasing). Between \( [[r1]] \) and \( [[r2]] \), exactly one factor is positive so \( f'>0 \) (increasing) -- so \( [[r1]] \) is a local MIN. Between \( [[r2]] \) and \( [[r3]] \), exactly two factors are positive so \( f'<0 \) (decreasing) -- so \( [[r2]] \) is a local MAX. For \( x>[[r3]] \), all factors are positive (increasing), making \( [[r3]] \) a local min. So the local maximum is at \( x=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the smaller outer root, which is a local minimum, not a maximum.
This is the larger outer root, which is also a local minimum, not a maximum.
This averages the two outer roots instead of identifying the middle critical point.
This adds all three roots together instead of picking the local maximum.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: For \( x>0 \), let \( f(x)=x+\dfrac{[[k]]}{x} \). On what interval is \( f \) increasing? Give the left endpoint of that interval.
solution: \( f'(x)=1-\dfrac{[[k]]}{x^{2}}=0 \) at \( x=\sqrt{[[k]]} \) (for \( x>0 \)); \( f'>0 \) for \( x>\sqrt{[[k]]} \), so \( f \) increases on \( (\sqrt{[[k]]},\infty) \), with left endpoint \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take a square root.
This halves the value under the root instead of taking its square root.
This is the negative root, outside the domain \( x>0 \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
